Extensive experiments on the Building3D dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, establish- ing diffusion-based approaches as a promising direction for wireframe reconstruction.

Our contributions are summarized in the following: • We introduce EdgeDiff, the first noise-to-wireframe paradigm that leverages diffusion models for progressive wireframe reconstruction through iterative structure re- finement. • We develop a novel edge-aware denoising module incor- porating an edge attention generation module, which en- hances the denoising process by capturing fine-grained edge cues from point clouds. • Comprehensive experiments conducted on the Build- ing3D dataset demonstrate that EdgeDiff achieves state- of-the-art performance, showing the effectiveness of our noise-to-wireframe paradigm.

Extensive experiments on the Build- ing3D dataset demonstrate that EdgeDiff significantly out- performs existing methods while maintaining geometric fi- delity, validating the effectiveness of diffusion model for structured 3D reconstruction tasks.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Building reconstruction is a challenging problem at the in-
tersection of computer vision, photogrammetry and com-
puter graphics. 3D wireframe presents a compelling repre-
sentation for building modeling through its compact struc-
ture. Existing wireframe reconstruction methods employ-
ing vertex detection and edge regression have achieved
promising results. In this paper, we develop an Edge-
aware Diffusion network, dubbed EdgeDiff. As a novel
paradigm for wireframe reconstruction, the EdgeDiff gen-
erates wireframe models from noise using a conditional
diffusion model. During the training process, the ground
truth wireframes firstly are formulated as a set of param-
eterized edges and then diffused into a random noise dis-
tribution. EdgeDiff learns both the noise reversal process
and the network structure simultaneously. During infer-
ence, EdgeDiff iteratively refines the generated edge dis-
tribution using the denoising diffusion implicit model, en-
abling flexible single- or multi-step denoising and dynamic
adaptation to buildings of varying complexity. Additionally,
given the unique structure of wireframes, we introduce an
edge attention module to extract point-wise attention from
point features, using it as auxiliary information to facilitate
learning of edge cues and guide the network toward im-
proved edge awareness. Extensive experiments on the real-
world Building3D dataset demonstrate that our approach
achieves state-of-the-art performance.

1. Introduction
Building reconstruction has emerged as a central chal-
lenge in computer vision and photogrammetry [9, 16], with
far-reaching implications across domains including smart
cities, autonomous navigation, and augmented and virtual
reality. Among various geometric representations for build-
ing modeling, like point clouds [7], meshes [8], and signed

Figure 1. Comparison of building wireframe reconstruction
paradigms. Different from existing methods based on vertex de-
tection (a) and edge regression (b), EdgeDiff (c) proposes a novel
noise-to-wireframe paradigm that iteratively refines edge parame-
ters through edge-aware denoising.
distance functions [4, 36], 3D wireframes [31, 32, 44] ex-
hibit unique advantages through its compact yet expressive
point-line structure. However, reconstructing accurate wire-
frames from real-world point cloud data remains a challeng-
ing problem, primarily due to noise, incompleteness, and
irregular sampling in the input measurements.
Deep learning-based methods [14, 23, 26, 55] have
shown tremendous potential in building wireframe recon-
struction. Existing approaches can be broadly categorized
into two paradigms: vertex detection methods [23, 26] and
edge regression methods [14, 55]. The former first detects

Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;

17008

vertices from point clouds and then predicts the topology
of edges (See Fig. 1 (a)). While this decomposition ap-
pears intuitive, it inherently leads to error accumulation
across stages. This insight has driven the emergence of
edge regression methods [14, 55], which employ learnable
queries to directly regress parameterized edges (See Fig. 1
(b)). Edge regression methods demonstrate superior stabil-
ity and accuracy by effectively addressing the aforemen-
tioned limitations. This paradigm evolution reveals that
wireframe reconstruction strategies are shifting from cue
search inference methods toward regression-based methods
for edge distributions. Note that popular diffusion models,
as a unique distribution regression strategy, have demon-
strated stronger distribution regression capabilities on im-
ages compared to methods based on standard transformers.
Motivated by this, we first propose a wireframe reconstruc-
tion paradigm based on diffusion models, which iteratively
refines the edge distribution from a noisy edge distribution,
as shown in Fig. 1 (c).
The popular diffusion models [11, 35, 41] have emerged
as a groundbreaking framework for generative modeling,
demonstrating remarkable capabilities through their pro-
gressive denoising process. The exceptional performance
of diffusion model in high-fidelity image synthesis [19, 39]
has catalyzed their adoption across diverse 2D vision tasks,
notably in object detection [2, 28, 48] and dense prediction
[17, 22]. Motivated by these advances, researchers have
extended diffusion-based approaches to 3D vision tasks,
where the inherent geometric complexity of point cloud rep-
resentation introduces unique challenges and opportunities.
Pioneering works in point cloud completion [21, 29] and
shape generation [24, 42] have demonstrated the effective-
ness of noise-guided structure modeling in 3D space, high-
lighting the potential of diffusion models for complex geo-
metric tasks. These successes can be attributed to two fun-
damental strengths of diffusion models: their capacity to
model complex data distributions through controlled noise
perturbation, and their iterative refinement mechanism that
inherently aligns with progressive geometric understanding.
Inspired by these observations, the EdgeDiff based on
conditional diffusion models is proposed for wireframe re-
construction (See Fig. 1 (c)). Our key insight is to re-
formulate wireframe reconstruction as a structured gener-
ative process that progressively evolves from random noise
to precise geometric representations. We first parameter-
ize wireframe using six-dimensional edge representations
that include the midpoints of the edges and their offsets.
During training, these parameterized edges undergo con-
trolled diffusion into gaussian distributions. EdgeDiff then
learns to reverse this process by iteratively refining noisy
edges into accurate geometric structures, guided by point
cloud. At inference stage, EdgeDiff enables flexible single-
or multi-step denoising and dynamic adaptation to vary-
ing edge numbers, generating final wireframes from ran-
dom initializations. Additionally, considering the crucial
role of conditioning signals in diffusion models [39, 52], we
leverage edge points as geometric cues [18, 47] and intro-
duce an edge attention generation module that incorporates
point-wise attention for edge-aware denoising. Extensive
experiments on the Building3D dataset demonstrate that our
approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, establish-
ing diffusion-based approaches as a promising direction for
wireframe reconstruction.
Our contributions are summarized in the following:
• We introduce EdgeDiff, the first noise-to-wireframe
paradigm that leverages diffusion models for progressive
wireframe reconstruction through iterative structure re-
finement.
• We develop a novel edge-aware denoising module incor-
porating an edge attention generation module, which en-
hances the denoising process by capturing fine-grained
edge cues from point clouds.
• Comprehensive experiments conducted on the Build-
ing3D dataset demonstrate that EdgeDiff achieves state-
of-the-art performance, showing the effectiveness of our
noise-to-wireframe paradigm.
raw_sections.method:
3. Method
We first introduce preliminaries and problem formulation
in Sec. 3.1. The proposed noise-to-wireframe paradigm and
key components of EdgeDiff are then elaborated in Sec. 3.2,
followed by the training and inference procedures detailed
in Sec. 3.3 and Sec. 3.4.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this work, we propose EdgeDiff, the first diffusion-
based framework that reformulates wireframe reconstruc-
tion as a noise-to-wireframe generation process, which is
a novel wireframe reconstruction paradigm. Our approach
exhibits several appealing properties, including edge at-
tention guidance for accurate structure learning and dy-
namic inference. Extensive experiments on the Build-
ing3D dataset demonstrate that EdgeDiff significantly out-
performs existing methods while maintaining geometric fi-
delity, validating the effectiveness of diffusion model for
structured 3D reconstruction tasks. Future research will ex-
plore more concise frameworks that directly generate global
wireframes without relying on post-processing steps.
Acknowledgement. This work is supported by the Na-
tional Key Research and Development Program of China
(2022YFB2602105), the Key Technological Innovation
Program of Ningbo City under Grant No. 2024Z297, and
the Technical Service Project for Real-Scene 3D Guangxi
Construction under Grant No. KWBD5C2024025.
